items.title: Email spam filter
items.scenario: A bank trains logistic regression on 50k labeled emails (spam=1, ham=0) with features like keyword counts and sender reputation. For a new message, the model outputs ŷ = 0.92. With threshold 0.5, it is classified spam; the security team can also quarantine anything above 0.8 without waiting for a hard label.
items.explanation: Binary classification with probabilistic output. BCE training pushes ŷ toward 1 on spam and toward 0 on ham. The sigmoid keeps scores in (0, 1) for ranking and threshold tuning.

items.title: Medical screening (diabetes risk)
items.scenario: Clinic features: age, BMI, fasting glucose. Logistic regression estimates P(diabetes=1|x). A patient with ŷ = 0.15 is low risk; another with ŷ = 0.78 triggers follow-up testing even though both might be labeled correctly at the 0.5 threshold.
items.explanation: Probabilities support risk stratification beyond a single cutoff. Calibration and threshold choice matter clinically — evaluation often uses AUC, not accuracy alone.

items.title: Credit default prediction
items.scenario: Lender uses income, debt ratio, and payment history. Training minimizes BCE. A loan applicant with ŷ = 0.48 vs 0.52 differs by only 0.04 probability but flips the default 0.5 decision — motivating cost-sensitive thresholds and AUC-based model comparison.
items.explanation: Linear decision boundary in feature space; business costs rarely make 0.5 optimal. Logistic regression gives a baseline probabilistic scorer before trying complex models.

items.title: Marketing click-through (CTR)
items.scenario: Ad features predict P(click=1|impression). Model A: average BCE 0.38 on validation. Model B: 0.35 — better probabilistic fit even if both hit 71% accuracy at threshold 0.5.
items.explanation: BCE rewards well-calibrated probabilities, not just correct argmax labels. Two models with identical accuracy can differ in BCE when confidence on correct classes differs.
